Week 4:
Critical Data
Studies

MDA10012: Communicating with Data


Dr Kyle Moore

  This Weeks
  Lecture



                Critical Data Studies (CDS)



                Data is a form of Power



                Critiquing Power Structures











Critical Data Studies                                                                                    2

    “Critical Data Studies seeks to
    identify how the epistemological
    and ontological implications of
    data collection and data          -driven
    processes may (re)constitute
    both knowledge and subjectivity”

    (Hintz et al.2019, p.6)











Critical Data Studies                                           3

   What do we mean
   by critical?



        Critical data studies is concerned with the
        significance (and power) of digital data in
        contemporary society and how it relates to
        societal transformation (Heep     and Kramp,
        2022)


        Critical does not mean to directly ‘criticise’
        but rather, to reflect on context and validity


        It is a means of interrogating or asking
        questions about specific information or
        phenomena.



Critical Data Studies                                                                                                      4

   Critical Data Studies:
   Key Questions
        How does data alter reality?
        i.e. how does it shape our knowledge, social world, etc
        What are the values of data?
        i.e. who benefits from data? What value does it create, economic,
        social, or otherwise, and for who?
        What shifts in power are associated with data?
        i.e. how is data used within systems of power? For who?
        What are the social/political consequences of our data-driven
        society?
        i.e. does this differ from any other forms of control –        if so – how?








Selwyn, N. (2019, March 11). ‘Critical Data Studies’ – four key questions. Enhancing the Use of Digital Data in Secondary Schools. https://data-5
smart-schools.net/2019/03/11/critical-data-studies-four-key-questions/

Critical Data
Studies

Critical Data studies investigates the structural
barriers surrounding people and data
What agency topeople have overtheirown  data?





                                                              6

Data is a form
of power

This is the underlying basis for Critical Data Studies
Ifwe accept this, we can start to explore these
issues alongside existing power structures





                                                                7

   Data Feminism






         Data feminism begins with a belief in gender
         equality, and a recognition that achieving
         equality for people of all genders requires a
         commitment to examining the root cause of
         the inequalities that certain groups face
         today


         Data Feminism is an intersectional
         approach, looking at the relationship
         between data, power, gender, class, race,
         sexuality, ability, age, religion, geography,
         etc.





Critical Data Studies                                                                                                              8

    Seven Principles
    of Data Feminism

1.   Examine power. Data feminism begins by analysing how power operates in the world.
2.   Challenge power. Data feminism commits to challenging unequal power structures and working
     toward justice.
3.   Elevate emotion and embodiment. Data feminism teaches us to value multiple forms of
     knowledge, including the knowledge that comes from people as living, feeling bodies in the
     world.
4.   Rethink binaries and hierarchies. Data feminism requires us to challenge the gender binary,
     along with other systems of counting and classification that perpetuate oppression.
5.   Embrace pluralism. Data feminism insists that the most complete knowledge comes from
     synthesising multiple perspectives, with priority given to local, Indigenous, and experiential
     ways of knowing.
6.   Consider context. Data feminism asserts that data is not neutral or objective. It is the product of
     unequal social relations, and this context is essential for conducting accurate, ethical analysis.
7.   Make labour visible. The work of data science, like all work in the world, is the work of many
     hands. Data feminism makes this labour visible so that it can be recognised and valued.






Critical Data Studies                                                                                                                                               9

   Data Colonialism

        “Data colonialism combines the predatory
        extractive practices of historical colonialism
        with the abstract quantification methods of
        computing. Understanding Big Data from
        the Global South means understanding
        capitalism’s current dependence on this new
        type of appropriation that works at every
        point in space where people or things are
        attached to today’s infrastructures of
        connection.” (Couldry     and Mejias, 2019, p.
        337)
        This  can  occur  through:
        •   Data Capture -    Modes of Extraction
        •   Data-based judgements
        •   Colonised self and self via data






Critical Data Studies                                                                                                          10

   Data Colonialism






        •   We might also think of this as a form of
            digital colonialism
        •   This is the use of digital technology for
            political, economic, and social control
        •   There is a disconnection between
            ownership, control, and location of data
            vs users.
        •   There has been a push for “data
            sovereignty”, for countries and peoples
            to have control and ownership over their
            data






Critical Data Studies                                                                                                           11

   Data Colonialism:
   Power Structures



        “Similar to the technical architecture of
        classic colonialism, digital colonialism is
        rooted in the design of the tech ecosystem
        for the purposes of profit and plunder. If the
        railways and maritime trade routes were the
        “open veins” of the Global South back then,
        today, digital infrastructure takes on the
        same role: Big Tech corporations use
        proprietary software, corporate clouds, and
        centralised   Internet services to spy on
        users, process their data, and spit back
        manufactured services to subjects of their
        data fiefdoms.”  (Kwet, 2019)





Critical Data Studies                                                                                                        12

   Data Justice






        In an increasingly data-driven world, there
        needs to be ethically and fair frameworks for
        how people are made visible/invisible,
        represented, and treated, as a result of        data
        or data production
        Three   Pillars  of  Data  Justice:
        (in)visibility
        (dis)engagement with technology
        Antidiscrimination








Critical Data Studies                                                                                                           13

   Data Justice
   Defined



        “There are currently (at least) three main
        approaches to conceptualising        data justice:
        one addressing the ways in which data used
        for governance can support power
        asymmetries (Johnson, 2014), another
        focusing on the ways in which data
        technologies can provide greater distributive
        justice through making the poor visible
        (Heeks   and Renken, 2016) and another that
        is interested in how practices of
        dataveillance can impact on the work of
        social justice organisations    (Dencik   et al.,
        2016).”





Critical Data Studies                                                                                                         14

  Summary
  Understanding Data



        Data is never neutral or ‘raw’ –  data is social
        Data  literacy(understanding,   reading,
        writing,communicating)     isessential
        Critical data literacies are not just data skills,
        but understanding   data’s social context
        Data  isembedded     insocial systems and a
        part of our everyday culture
        CriticalData Studies interrogates the power
        in data systems and infrastructures








Critical Data Studies                                                                                                  15
